coredata.dc:title: Green synthesis of fluorescent carbon quantum dots from Eleusine coracana and their application as a fluorescence ‘turn-off’ sensor probe for selective detection of Cu2+
coredata.dc:creator: Murugan, N.
Prakash, M.
Jayakumar, M.
Sundaramurthy, Anandhakumar
Sundramoorthy, Ashok K.
coredata.dc:description: A facile, rapid and frugal approach was developed for the preparation of highly fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) by pyrolysis of Finger millet ragi (Eleusine coracana) as a carbon source. The optical properties of as-synthesized CQDs were analyzed by using UV-Visible and fluorescence spectroscopies. Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) showed the formation of CQDs that predominantly consists of carbonyl and hydroxyl groups which can provide more adsorption sites. The X-ray diffraction (XRD), High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) and Raman spectroscopy indicated that the synthesized CQDs made of amorphous graphitic carbon like structure. The size distribution was in the range of 3–8 nm with a high abundance of 6 nm particles. The CQDs exhibited bright blue fluorescence emission (wavelength ∼425 nm) under UV-light with an excitation wavelength of 340 nm. Our study indicated that Cu2+ strongly quenched the fluorescent intensity of CQDs compared to other metal ions (Al3+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Mn2+, Cr3+ and Hg2+). There was a linear correlation between the quenching efficiency vs. concentration of Cu2+ ions in the range from 0 to 100 μM (R2 = 0.9918). Furthermore, CQDs have been applied in sensing of Cu2+ in real water samples with a detection limit (LOD) of 10 nM. In addition, density functional theory (DFT) calculations revealed that there was a strong interaction between CQDs with divalent metal ions whereas trivalent metal ions adsorbed weakly. Particularly, Ni2+ and Cu2+ formed a very strong bonding with CQDs compared to other divalent ions. The metal-CQDs complex structure was reported with optimized highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) – lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) energy gap. The nature of interaction and optical properties of the COOH-CQD’s have been studied with metal ion adsorbed on the surface. Charge analysis corroborated that there was a significant charge transfer between CQDs with Ni2+ and Cu2+. Our study revealed that Cu2+ preferentially get adsorbed on aromatic CC (π-bond) of CQDs whereas other divalent metals form σ-bond(s) with the CQDs. This observation further confirmed by the HOMO-LUMO band gap (Eg) and Frontier molecular orbital analysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Green synthesis of CQDs
Eleusine coracana
Carbon quantum dots
Cu2+ ion detection
Fluorescence sensor
DFT

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of metal ions by CQDs
UV–Vis, FT-IR and Zeta potential analysis
XRD and Raman characterizations of CQDs
Effect of temperature on CQDs fluorescence
Detection of Cu2+ ions in real water samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV-Vis spectrum of CQDs (insets visual images of CQDs under daylight (left) and UV irradiation (right). (b) FT-IR spectrum of synthesized CQDs, (c) XRD pattern and (d) Raman spectrum of CQDs.
(a) TEM images and (b) particle size distribution of CQDs. (c) HR-TEM images and (d) SAED pattern of CQDs.
XPS analysis of as-prepared CQDs. (a) XPS survey spectrum of the CQDs. (b) High resolution spectrum of C1s, (c) high resolution spectrum of O1s, and (d) high resolution spectrum of N1s.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of CQDs at different excitation wavelengths. (b) Excitation dependent fluorescence (emission) spectra. (c) Temperature dependent fluorescence spectra and (d) temperature dependent maximum emission wavelength at constant excitation of resulting CQDs.

(a) Fluorescence quenching efficiency of synthesized CQDs in the presence of different metal ions (concentration = 200 μM) at an excitation of 340 nm. (b) The difference in fluorescence intensity of CQDs dispersion between the blank and solutions containing various metal ions. (c) Fluorescence photographic images of CQDs with additions of various metal ions (Al3+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Pb2+, Mn2+, Cr3+, Hg2+, Ni2+ and Cu2+ 20 μM) under UV lamp at 365 nm.
Photographic images of Cu2+ ions in the presence of different Cu2+ concentrations (5 μM, 10 μM and 20 μM) under UV light excitation at 365 nm (Far left image was CQD under normal light).

(a) The fluorescence spectra of the solution in the presence of different concentrations of Cu2+ from 0 to 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80, 90 and 100 μM. (b) Calibration graph for the Cu2+ ions using the CQDs system. Error bars indicated the mean values obtained from the analysis of the triplicate measurements.

The fluorescence responses of the CQDs (excitation at 340 nm) dispersion with the different Cu2+ ions in (a and c) tap water and (b and d) river water samples within the range of (10 nM, 20 nM and 30 nM) (error bars indicate the mean values obtained from the analysis of the triplicate results).
Optimized geometries of CQD-Mn+ complexes at B3LYP/6-31+G** method along with the bond distances (in Å).
Frontier molecular orbitals (FMO) of CQD-Mn+ complexes at B3LYP/6-31+G** method along with the respective energy gap values (Eg, in eV).
Sensing principle of the CQDs based probe for Cu2+ ions detection.
CQDs based sensors for Cu2+ ions detection.
Calculated binding energies, band gap (Eg), metal ion charges and absorption spectral values at B3LYP/6-31+G** level of theory for surface adsorbed CQD’s-Mn + complexes.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: BSSE corrected binding energies with inclusion of dispersion correction term (+D3) at B3LYP/6-31+G** basis set.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: c
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: UV absorption range of different complexes and I-intense, M – medium and w – weak absorption peaks.

head.ce:title: Green synthesis of fluorescent carbon quantum dots from Eleusine coracana and their application as a fluorescence ‘turn-off’ sensor probe for selective detection of Cu2+

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, SRM Institute of Science and Technology, Kattankulathur 603 203, Tamil Nadu, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: SRM Institute of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: SRM Research Institute, SRM Institute of Science and Technology, Kattankulathur 603 203, Tamil Nadu, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: SRM Institute of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Electroplating and Metal Finishing Division, CSIR Central Electrochemical Research Institute, Karaikudi 630 006, Tamil Nadu, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Academy of Scientific and Innovative Research (AcSIR), CSIR Central Electrochemical Research Institute, Karaikudi 630 006, Tamil Nadu, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Academy of Scientific and Innovative Research (AcSIR)

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Chemistry, SRM Institute of Science and Technology, Kattankulathur 603 203, Tamil Nadu, India.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: SRM Institute of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A facile, rapid and frugal approach was developed for the preparation of highly fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) by pyrolysis of Finger millet ragi (Eleusine coracana) as a carbon source. The optical properties of as-synthesized CQDs were analyzed by using UV-Visible and fluorescence spectroscopies. Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) showed the formation of CQDs that predominantly consists of carbonyl and hydroxyl groups which can provide more adsorption sites. The X-ray diffraction (XRD), High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) and Raman spectroscopy indicated that the synthesized CQDs made of amorphous graphitic carbon like structure. The size distribution was in the range of 3–8 nm with a high abundance of 6 nm particles. The CQDs exhibited bright blue fluorescence emission (wavelength ∼425 nm) under UV-light with an excitation wavelength of 340 nm. Our study indicated that Cu2+ strongly quenched the fluorescent intensity of CQDs compared to other metal ions (Al3+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Mn2+, Cr3+ and Hg2+). There was a linear correlation between the quenching efficiency vs. concentration of Cu2+ ions in the range from 0 to 100 μM (R2 = 0.9918). Furthermore, CQDs have been applied in sensing of Cu2+ in real water samples with a detection limit (LOD) of 10 nM. In addition, density functional theory (DFT) calculations revealed that there was a strong interaction between CQDs with divalent metal ions whereas trivalent metal ions adsorbed weakly. Particularly, Ni2+ and Cu2+ formed a very strong bonding with CQDs compared to other divalent ions. The metal-CQDs complex structure was reported with optimized highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) – lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) energy gap. The nature of interaction and optical properties of the COOH-CQD’s have been studied with metal ion adsorbed on the surface. Charge analysis corroborated that there was a significant charge transfer between CQDs with Ni2+ and Cu2+. Our study revealed that Cu2+ preferentially get adsorbed on aromatic CC (π-bond) of CQDs whereas other divalent metals form σ-bond(s) with the CQDs. This observation further confirmed by the HOMO-LUMO band gap (Eg) and Frontier molecular orbital analysis.

ce:section.ce:para: Toxic heavy metal ions raise serious threat to the environment, human health and animals. There have been considerable efforts devoted to develop various methods to detect toxic metal ions such as Pb2+, Hg2+, Fe2+ and Cu2+. The monitoring of Cu2+ concentration has received increasing attention in toxicological research [1–4]. Copper is one of the most indispensable abundant crucial trace element in the human life system and commonly found in natural water [5]. Copper has a diverse role in biological process including regulation of hemoglobin levels, neuronal functions, mitochondrial respiration and cellular metabolism and so on [6]. Copper can affect liver or kidney upon long-term exposure with high concentrations and easily accumulate in human system. Cu was also linked to brain diseases such as Alzheimer’s and Parkinson’s. For these reasons, detection of Cu2+ has steadily attracted a great deal of attention in various fields [7,8].

Generally, heavy metal ions can be detected by inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry, [9] atomic absorption spectrometry, [10] atomic fluorescence spectrometry [11], potentiometry, [12] and electrochemical methods [13]. Some of these methods were found to be relatively expensive, complicated procedures involved that demand time consuming sample pretreatment process and non-portable. The fluorescence based sensors have attracted increasing attention due to its simplicity, good sensitivity with selectivity, faster response time, minimal interference effect and relatively low-cost [14].

Fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) exhibit several unique properties such as stable photoluminescence, water solubility, chemical inertness, less toxicity, good biocompatibility etc. CQDs, with sizes of <10 nm [15,16] have been demonstrated for various applications including photo-catalysis, cell imaging, fluorescent inks, medical diagnosis, detection of transition metal ions, chemicals sensor, biosensor and energy sources [17–20]. Different methods have been reported to prepare fluorescent CQDs, such as acidic oxidation, electrochemical synthesis, microwave/ultrasonic passivation, hydrothermal method, laser ablation, hot injection and pyrolysis [21–25]. In addition, various organic compounds have been utilized to synthesize CQDs using glucose [25], citric acid [26] and glycol [27].

However, most of the above methods have some limitations which includes utilizing harmful chemicals as carbon precursors, complicated synthetic procedures, and multiple synthesis purification steps [28,29]. From the environmental perspectives, there is a demand to use existing abundant natural materials to synthesize CQDs [30]. Natural materials broadly distributed in nature, easily accessible and available in limitless quantity, which is conducive to the development and application of CQDs. Recently, due to the environmental concerns, some alternative renewable resources were already reported for preparing CQDs [31,32] from sources like prawn shells [29], winter melon [30], blueberry [32], cotton [33], lychee seeds [34], sago waste [35], egg [36], peanut skin [37], hair [38], cucumber [39], peanut shells [40] and wheat straw[41].

Herein, we reported synthesis of highly fluorescent CQDs using Finger millet ragi (Eleusine coracana) [42]. As-derived CQDs contain oxygen rich hydroxyl group as it confirmed by FT-IR. Furthermore, CQDs were characterized by UV-Vis, Raman Spectroscopy, HR-TEM, XPS and XRD, respectively. The CQD dispersion was used for selective detection of Cu2+ ions by fluorescent spectroscopy in real water samples with a detection limit of 10 nM. According to the US Environmental Protection Agency (EPA), the maximum safe limit of Cu2+ concentration in drinking water should be less than 20 μM, so this method could be useful for detection of Cu2+ in real samples [43].

The Finger millet ragi (Eleusine coracana) was collected from local market (Chennai, India). CaCl2·2H2O, CuSO4·5H2O, Ni(NO3)2·6H2O, Mn(NO3)2·6H2O, MgCl2·6H2O, and CrCl3 were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich and PbSO4 , AlCl3 and HgCl2 were purchased from Avra Private Ltd. All the chemicals were used without prior purification unless otherwise noted. Aqueous solutions were prepared using distilled water and experiments were carried out at room temperature (28 ± 1 °C).

Crushed Eleusine coracana powder was washed with distilled water and dried in a hot air oven at 80 °C for 5 h. Dried samples were placed in high-temperature tubular furnace on a quartz boat crucible. The furnace temperature was gradually increased to 300 °C with the step of 5 °C/minand then maintained for 3 h. Then, it was allowed to cool down to room temperature, the obtained black products were grinded with a pestle and mortar to get a fine powder. 0.2 g of this product was ultrasonically dispersed in 20 ml of triple-distilled water to achieve a homogeneous dark brown solution. Finally, CQDs were collected by filtration using a (Whatman) filter paper (with pore sizes of 0.22 µm) and stored in a dark place at 5 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of metal ions by CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence spectra of CQDs were recorded in the presence and absences of different metal ions such as Al3+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Mn2+, Cr3+, and Hg2+ at the excited wavelength of 340 nm.

To demonstrate the practical application of synthesized CQDs, detection of Cu2+ ions in real water samples was demonstrated (i.e. Tap and river water). The tap water was collected from our laboratory (SRM IST, India) and palar river from Tamil Nadu, India. These samples were filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane (Millipore) and centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 15 min to remove any suspended particles. Later, both tap and river water samples were mixed with standard Cu2+ solutions (the concentration was from 10 to 30 nM) and the fluorescence spectra were recorded at room temperature (28 ± 1 °C).

The interactions and electronic transition between the CQD’s and various metal ions (M = Al3+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cr3+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Cu2+ and Zn2+) have been studied by using density functional theory (DFT) calculations. All metal complexes with COOH functionalized CQD’s were optimized at DFT based Becke’s three-parameter hybrid exchange functional and Lee–Yang–Parr correlation functional (B3LYP) using 6-31+G** basis set. Time dependent density functional theory (TDDFT) has been used to predict the absorption wavelength from optimized geometries of CQD and CQD-Mn+ (where M = Al3+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cr3+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Cu2+ and Zn2+; where n is the oxidation state of the different metal ions considered for the present work) complexes at B3LYP/6-31+G** method. Recent computational studies revealed that B3LYP method is more reliable for the structure stability and spectral prediction of CQD’s based composite materials [44,45]. All structures were optimized at solvent (i.e. Water) medium and frequency calculations are done at the same level of theory to ensure the all complexes in minima in the potential energy surfaces. Polarizable continuum model (PCM) has been used to quantify the solvent effect on the structure and UV-Vis absorption spectra of CQD-Mn+ complexes. All electronic structure calculations were performed using GAUSSIAN 16 and Gauss View 6.0 for visualization [46].

The binding energy (BE) of the complexes were calculated using the supramolecular approach and corrected for basis set superposition error (BSSE) employing the counterpoise (CP) procedure suggested by Boys and Bernardi [47] in Eq. (1),
(1)
BE =
E
Complex
-
∑
i = 1
n
E i
where EComplex is the total energy of the complex, Ei is energy of the monomer (i.e. CQDs and Mn+ atoms). We deduced the energetics with and without inclusion of Grimme’s (DFT-D3) [48] dispersion correction in order to quantify the role of dispersion energy in these complexes.

UV-Vis absorption spectra were recorded by using Perkin Elmer spectrophotometer. Fourier-transform infrared spectrometer (FT-IR, Impact 400 D Nicholet Spectrometer) was employed to substantiate the presence of functional groups. Powder XRD patterns were carried out by using a diffractometer (Bruker D8 Advance diffractometer) at operating voltage of 40 kV with a scan rate of 0.02°. Raman spectra were obtained on a Raman Spectrometer with laser wavelength of 633 nm using a (Renishaw Raman scope) in back scattering configuration. The surface morphology of CQDs were investigated by Transmission electron microscope (TEM) performed with a FEI-Tecnai F20 microscope. Zeta potential measurements were carried out using Nanotrac Wave II; Microtrac Inc., USA. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (Make: Thermo Scientific, Model: a MULTILAB 2000 Base system with X-ray, Auger and ISS attachments) and the high resolution TEM (HR-TEM) JEM-2100 Plus Electron Microscope, Japan were used for characterizations. The Photoluminescence spectra were recorded by using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) under ambient conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV–Vis, FT-IR and Zeta potential analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The UV–vis absorption spectrum of CQDs is shown in Fig. 1 (a). This spectrum exhibits two absorption peaks, first one at 230 nm (sharp absorption peak) which is assigned to then π–π* transition of CC bonds of aromatic rings, and second absorption peak at 305 nm which was assigned to the n–π* transitions of CO bonds [49]. As shown in inset of Fig. 1(a), the diluted CQDs solution was light brown in color under normal light, but it turned to bright blue under UV light at 365 nm. This UV–Vis data confirmed the formation of CQDs from ragi [50].

FT-IR spectrum presents the functional groups of the CQDs. Fig. 1(b) shows the IR band within the range of 3450–2950 cm−1 which was attributed to OH vibration and stretching of NH bands of phenolic and amine groups. The peaks at 2932 cm−1 and 2829 cm−1 are assigned to CH stretching vibration. Moreover, the absorption peaks at 1655 cm−1 indicated the presence of carboxyl (CO) functional groups for synthesized CQDs [3]. The absorption peaks at 1153 cm−1 and 1013 cm−1 indicated the presence of CN stretching and ether linkage respectively (Fig. 1b) [51]. This data suggested that the synthesized CQDs contained OH, (NH) and (CO) groups. Thus, all these characteristic functional groups obtained for CQDs (ragi) corroborated the formation of water soluble CQDs [52]. In addition, Zeta potential analysis of diluted CQDs solution was performed and it was found to be –23.5 ± 1.5 mV, which confirmed the presence of negatively charged carboxyl and hydroxyl functional groups on the surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: XRD and Raman characterizations of CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The XRD data of CQDs is shown in Fig. 1(c) which exhibited two diffraction peaks at 2θ = 25° (intense peak) and a weak peak at 2θ = 42° that were assigned to the (0 0 2) and (1 0 0) diffraction patterns of graphitic carbon respectively, indicating that synthesized CQDs were an amorphous in nature [53]. This result indicated the poor crystalline nature of the CQDs due to the attachment of functional groups. In addition, as it confirmed by FT-IR, the presence of oxygen and amine functional groups on CQDs were also evident for amorphous nature of CQDs [54].

Raman spectroscopy is a powerful tool for the characterization of edge defects, disorder and in plane crystalline size of graphitic carbon. Raman spectrum of CQDs exhibits two distinct bands at 1360 cm−1 (disordered D band) and the crystalline band at 1592 cm−1 (G bands) respectively. As shown in Fig. 1d, D band represents sp3 defects (hybridized) whereas G band corresponds to the sp2 carbons (hybridized) [55]. This intensity ratio between disordered D band and the crystalline G band of CQDs, indicated that they have a similar structure to graphene oxide. The presence of D band on CQDs is also confirmed that this structure contains functional groups which could be related to its amorphous nature [56].

The morphology and crystal structure of prepared CQDs were analyzed by HR-TEM. As shown in Fig. 2 a, CQDs were appeared as spherical, amorphous in nature and well dispersed within range from ∼3 to 8 nm. These particle sizes were estimated from a TEM image (Fig. 2b). The HR-TEM image revealed a well-resolved lattice structure fringes which corresponds to the planes of graphitic carbon like structure (Fig. 2c) [57]. This graphitic like structure may be due to the presence of sp2 carbon (0 0 2) plane, which was in good agreement with the XRD peak obtained at 2θ = 25°. The selective area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern exhibits a well-distinguishable concentric ring pattern representing the planes of graphitic like structure (Fig. 2d). This result further confirmed the successful synthesis of small spherical CQDs.

XPS was carried out to ascertain the chemical state and functional groups present on the surface. Survey spectrum of as synthesized CQDs depicted in Fig. 3 a, shows peaks corresponding to C, O and N at 285, 532 and 401 eV, respectively. Fig. 3b shows the spectral envelope of C1s could be de-convoluted into 2 peaks, at 284.8 and 285.5 eV which can be assigned to adventitious sp2 or CC bonded carbon, and to CO/CN species, respectively. Fig. 3c shows the deconvoluted high-resolution spectrum of O1s. This can be assigned to two peaks at 531.7 eV (CO) and 533.3 eV (CO) bonds, indicating the presence of hydroxyl, carbonyl and carboxylic acid groups on the CQDs surface [58,59]. Fig. 3d shows the N1s spectral signal observed at 400.9 eV indicated the presence of low content carbon bonded nitrogen (CN) in CQDs.

Fig. 4 (a) shows the excitation-dependent emission spectra of the synthesized CQDs with increasing excitation from 300 to 400 nm. The strongest emission was obtained at 340 nm excitation with the emission wavelength of 425 nm, respectively. The fluorescence intensity gradually increased with the excitation wavelengths from 300 to 340 nm and then decreased with the increase of excitation wavelengths from 350 to 420 nm. The excitation dependent emission spectra and corresponding wavelengths were presented in Fig. 4(b). When the excitation wavelengths changed from 300 to 420 nm, the emission wavelength enhanced from 407 to 497 nm which may be because of different sizes of CQDs or distribution of different surface states and functional groups or various surface energy traps on the CQDs. These reasons were emissive traps, exactions of carbon, triplet carbenes at the zigzag edges, aromatic conjugate structures, and attached functional groups (carboxyl, amide and oxygen rich hydroxyl groups) [60].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of temperature on CQDs fluorescence
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence intensity of CQDs was examined at different temperatures (Fig. 4c). The fluorescence intensity decreased with temperature changes from 20 to 100 °C, without significant change in the emission wavelength (Fig. 4d). This data confirmed the good stability of CQDs dispersion and its photoluminescence properties at different temperatures. This observation indicated that as-synthesized CQDs can be applied for various temperature dependent applications.

We further evaluated the ability of CQDs as a fluorescent probe for analytical applications. Different metal ions (Al3+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Mn2+, Cr3+, and Hg2+ each at a concentration of 200 µM) and their effects on the fluorescent intensity of CQDs were recorded after metal ions addition at room temperature. The selectivity of the sensing system was evaluated. We observed different quenching effects with various metal ions. Among them, Cu2+ ion remarkably selectively quenched the fluorescence intensity of CQD as compared with the other metal ions as shown in Fig. 5 (a). The histogram was plotted in the presence of (200 μM) various metal ions by taking a relationship between the quenching ratio (F/F0) against equal concentration of the different metal ions, of which Cu2+ exhibits the lowest value. The emission spectra of CQDs did not show noticeable changes for any of other metal ions Fig. 5 (b), indicating that there was no energy transfer between these metal ions and CQDs. The ratio of F and F0 indicated the FL intensity at 340 nm in presence and absence of different metal ions. This ratio suggested that there was strong affinity between CQDs and Cu2+, so it could be used as an efficient fluorescence probe for Cu2+ ions. The interaction between CQDs and Cu2+ ions might have attributed to the quenching of the fluorescence by electron or energy transfer. Fig. 5 (c) shows the visual images of various metal ions (Al3+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Pb2+, Mn2+, Cr3+, Hg2+, Ni2+ and Cu2+ 20 μM) with CQDs dispersion under UV light at 365 nm. This image showed a remarkable fluorescence quenching (color of the solution was changed from dark blue to light blue fluorescence) for only Cu2+ compared with the other metal cations. This observation suggested that our synthesized fluorescent CQDs could be used as a selective indicator sensor for Cu2+ ions.

Further, Fig. 6 shows the photographic images of CQDs (blank) dispersion with additions Cu2+ ions under visible light and UV-light (365 nm). Cu2+ concentrations varied from 5, 10 and 20 μM into CQDs. These photographic images were taken for the solutions after waiting time of two minutes. As we could see, fluorescence intensity drastically changed from bright blue to light blue with the concentration of Cu2+. It is highly possible to use CQD as a visual indicator for Cu2+ ions.

We further evaluated the sensitivity of CQDs for various concentrations of Cu2+ ions from 0 to 100 μM (Fig. 7 a). Quenching of fluorescence intensity (turn off) with increasing concentration of Cu2+ ions did not shift the emission peak. The FL intensity clearly weakened after each addition of Cu2+. It was due to chelation of Cu2+ ion with the carboxyl group and carbonyl group at the surface of the CQDs. Fig. 7(b) shows the calibration curve obtained for Cu2+ concentration vs. fluorescence intensity (error bars indicated the mean values obtained from the analysis of the triplicate measurements). As shown, there was a good linear correlation between the quenching efficiency against concentration of Cu2+ ions in the range from 0 to 100 μM (R2 = 0.9918).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Cu2+ ions in real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: To further verify the feasibility of the proposed method for the determination of Cu2+ ions in real water samples, the spiked samples were prepared with known concentration of Cu2+ ions from 10 to 30 nM. CQD dispersion exhibited a well (turn-off) fluorescence response towards Cu2+ ions as shown in Fig. 8 (a & b). The fluorescence intensity of CQDs dramatically decreased with the increasing concentration of Cu2+ ions in tap and river water samples. The lower concentration of 10 nM (Cu2+) was detected accurately which indicated that our sensing system is highly sensitive to Cu2+. The limit of detection was evaluated to be 10 nM (Fig. 8c and d). We have also compared detection limit, and linear range of present method with the other reported CQDs based sensors in Table 1 . As we can see, this proposed method offers several advantages such as low detection limit and comparable wide linear range. The maximum safe limit of Cu2+ in drinking water should be no more than 20 μM as set by the US (EPA) [61], so we believe that our proposed method can be applied for the detection of Cu2+ ions.

The abundant oxygen-containing functional groups present in CQDs as COOH, OH and NH groups might contribute for strong affinity to Cu2+ ions. This allows the CQDs to serve as a fluorescent probe with excellent sensitivity and selectivity for Cu2+ by formation of a complex (Scheme 1 ) [62,63]. FL quenching mechanism might attribute through inner filter effect, energy or electron transfer process. Here, Cu2+ acted as an electron accepter and CQDs possess a variety of functional groups such as π-systems, hydroxyl, carbonyl and amino groups. Cu2+ is a paramagnetic ion with d9 system having one unoccupied d shell, for this reason Cu2+ ions can be easily adsorbed onto the surface which leads to the effective quenching of the fluorescence [29].

The calculated BE (Table 2) values reveal that there was a strong covalent interaction between CQD and Cu2+ and Ni2+ ions. The corresponding Eg value and charges of the metal ion on CQD also very low when compared with the other metal ions. The calculated HOMO-LUMO gap is directly correlated with the charge of metal ion on surface. The lower Eg value directly correlated with the charge of the metal at surface. For example the Eg value for Cu2+ is 1.14 eV and respective metal charge is 0.451 a.u. Similarly, Eg for Ni2+ is 1.49 and respective charge is 0.401 a.u. This observation clearly reveals that lower the band gap favor for the charge transfer between the complexes. This is also in good agreement with the BEs of the metal complexes.

To understand the effect of functionalization and metal ion charges, frontier molecular orbital analysis is the main tool to predict the electronic and optical transition between metal ions and CQD-Mn+ complexes. The optimized geometries and surface densities of the frontier molecular orbitals (FMO) of CQD with Mn+ complexes were shown in Figs. 9 and 10 along with the HOMO-LUMO energy gap (Eg, in eV). It was found from our studies, π to π* transition was dominated in CQD, as confirmed from the FMO. When CQD interact with Mn+ complex, HOMO of most of the complexes localized in CQD and LUMO was always in the metal ion for the weakly adsorbed metal complexes. Whereas, for strongly bonded metal complexes, it behaves differently. For example, HOMO-LUMO+2 transition have been observed for CQD-Ni2+ and CQD-Cu2+ complexes. Here, HOMO orbital localized in carbon plane and LUMO+1 on metal ion. It was interesting to note that in CQD-Cu2+ complex, the HOMO/LUMO transition in FMO was different from other metal complex. Here, major transition occurs from HOMO to LUMO and LUMO to LUMO+1. The geometrical parameters of Cu2+ complex confirm the same with the adsorption site of metal ion which is different from the other metals. It is worth to note that Cu2+ ion exactly binds on the π-bond on surface whereas other metals form an σ-bond. The binding nature of Cu2+ on CQD surface is different from other metal ion, and this may affect the π-π* transition in carbon surface.

HOMO-LUMO gap always depends on the functional groups present in the nanostructures and the nature of metal and oxidation states of metal ions. Our study showed that the HOMO–LUMO gap of the CQD-metal complexes was lower when compared to carboxylate CQD. It is interesting to note that the HOMO–LUMO Eg for the Co2+, Ni2+ and Cu2+ complexes are significantly less when compared to the other metal complexes and COOH-CQD (i.e. ∼3.68 eV). The respective Eg for these complexes was varied from 1.1 to 1.49 eV. Whereas same value for the divalent Zn2+ complex was ∼2.40 eV, which was a moderate Eg value when compared to the respective divalent metal ions. The Eg value for the other divalent metal ion was 3.68 eV (Ca2+), 3.69 eV (Mg2+), 1.75 eV (Mn2+), and 2.40 eV (Zn2+). The lower energy gap arises from the functionalization of the CQDs and hybridization of metal ion with CQDs. A similar observation had been found in functionalized graphene quantum dots [64–66].

Our calculation reveals that the lower energy gap was due to the effect of perfect hybridization of divalent metal (Cu2+) ion with CQD. When compared with Zn2+, charge transfer in Cu2+ complex was very perfect and charge transfer process quenched by these HOMO-LUMO orbitals (see Fig. 10). Our calculated geometries and FMOs were in good agreement with the experimental data. This data might be helpful to design novel functional materials for various electronic applications.

Moreover, the UV-Vis spectra of CQDs with different metal atoms were being analyzed and the most intense peaks (λmax) for COOH functionalized, Ca2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Cu2+ and Zn2+ were 330, 327, 330, 358, 323, 701 and 317 nm, respectively. From our calculations; all metal complexes exhibited absorption bands in the range of 226–340 nm except Cu2+-CQD. This is due to the nature of hybridization and overlapping orbitals between metal – CQD complexes. This may be affecting the composite stability and the electronic properties of the complexes. Our calculated absorption bands were in good agreement with experimental UV-Vis spectrum. Our studies could provide valuable information to design functional materials for electronic and optical applications.

In summary, we have reported a simple, economic and green method to produce water-soluble CQDs with strong fluorescence from ragi via a single step pyrolysis method. This method offers numerous advantages over other synthetic techniques. The obtained CQDs exhibited good photoluminescence stability, high water solubility and emission behavior. CQDs were analyzed by using Raman, UV-Vis and fluorescence spectroscopy. HR-TEM, FT-IR and XPS characterizations clearly showed the formation of CQDs that predominantly consists of carbonyl groups and hydroxyl groups. The XRD and TEM analysis indicated that the synthesized CQDs possess amorphous graphitic carbon like structure with a narrow size distribution from 3–8 nm. The synthesized CQDs were analyzed by Raman spectroscopy, which confirmed the presence of defects and amorphous nature (due to functional groups) of CQDs. Moreover, these CQDs could be used as an efficient fluorescent probe for the detection of Cu2+ ions with good selectivity and sensitivity with a detection limit of 10 nM. In addition, DFT BE calculations revealed that there was a strong interaction between CQDs with divalent metal ions whereas trivalent metal ions adsorbed weakly. FMO and charge analysis reveals that there was significant charge transfer between metal ions and CQDs which also proved a preferential interaction between Cu2+ and CQDs. Close analysis of our results indicated that Cu2+ preferentially get adsorbed on aromatic CC (π-bond) of CQDs whereas other divalent metals form σ-bond(s) with the CQDs. We believe that this new method can be used for detection of Cu2+ ion by using fluorescence spectroscopy and various other applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is partially supported from the Science and Engineering Research Board, New Delhi, India (Ref. No.: ECR/2016/001446). N.M acknowledges the postdoctoral fellowship from SRM Institute of Science and Technology. Furthermore, we acknowledge the financial support for “HR-TEM and XRD facilities” from the Government of India (MNRE Project No. 31/03/2014-15/PVSE-R&D). Authors thank SRM Supercomputer Centre, SRM Institute of Science and Technology for providing the computational facility.
